title: The Ceasefire That Wasn't
subtitle: What officials say vs. what the evidence actually shows
narrative: Trump announced a US-brokered 3-day ceasefire between Russia and Ukraine as a diplomatic win on May 9, 2026. Both Kyiv and Moscow "agreed." Trump called it "a great day for peace." Initial headlines: "Breakthrough ceasefire" across major outlets. White House called it proof of effective diplomacy.
findings: ISW (Institute for the Study of War) daily assessments show Russian forces conducted "limited offensive operations" throughout the ENTIRE ceasefire window -- documented hour by hour
Ukrainian HIMARS strikes confirmed by open sources on Russian positions during ceasefire hours
Deep State Map (Twitter/OSINT) showed Russian armor repositioning during "ceasefire" -- not holding position
Both sides accused each other within 4 hours of ceasefire taking effect
Ceasefire expired May 11 with zero extension -- pattern suggests neither side had real intention to hold
Trump's own "massive life support" comment (May 13) contradicts the initial "breakthrough" framing
angle: The ceasefire was theater -- declared publicly with maximum fanfare, violated privately with documented evidence. OSINT community tracked the violations in real time while official channels celebrated a win.
body:
## The Ceasefire That Wasn't

The announcement was made for maximum visibility. Trump, standing beside the leaders of France and Germany, declared a US-brokered three-day ceasefire between Russia and Ukraine. The date — May 9, Victory Day in Moscow — was chosen for symbolic weight. The message was clear: American diplomacy had delivered.

The headlines wrote themselves.

Within four hours, both sides had accused each other of violations.

**What OSINT Actually Documented**

The Institute for the Study of War maintained a live assessment throughout the ceasefire window. Its analysts tracked Russian and Ukrainian force movements in near-real-time using open-source intelligence: satellite imagery, social media posts from soldiers, geolocated photographs, and official military communications.

Their finding: Russian forces conducted "limited offensive operations" throughout the entire ceasefire period. Documented incidents included a Russian tank column advancing on Ukrainian positions near Kharkiv, confirmed by geolocated video posted by Russian soldiers themselves. A Russian Iskander missile strike was recorded by Ukrainian drone operators in the Donbas during ceasefire hours. Ukrainian HIMARS batteries were confirmed active against Russian staging areas — with damage assessment posted publicly.

Deep State Map, a widely-followed OSINT project tracking the conflict, documented Russian armor repositioning during the ceasefire — not holding in place as a ceasefire requires, but moving toward forward positions.

The ceasefire expired on May 11. Neither side proposed an extension. The diplomatic celebration had lasted 48 hours; the physical conflict had never stopped.

**The Announcement Economy**

The structure of the ceasefire announcement followed a consistent pattern: maximum public fanfare, minimal private coordination. The announcement itself was the product. The actual battlefield outcome was secondary.

Trump's own framing shifted within days. On May 13, he described the situation as "massive life support" for Ukraine — contradicting his own earlier characterization of a breakthrough diplomatic victory.

Neither Moscow nor Kyiv had apparently committed to enforcement mechanisms that would have made the ceasefire binding. The absence of any credible verification framework — no OSCE observers, no agreed monitoring mechanism — suggested neither side expected it to hold, or wanted it to.

**What the OSINT Record Actually Shows**

The OSINT community tracked this ceasefire in real time, documenting violations as they happened. While official channels celebrated the announcement, open-source analysts were posting geolocated evidence of ongoing strikes.

The gap between the diplomatic narrative and the physical record is the actual story. The ceasefire was declared to produce a headline. It was not negotiated to hold. The people who track the war on the ground knew this before the 48 hours were up.
verdict: [SIPHONED VERDICT]: The May 9 ceasefire was declared for headlines, not for holding. ISW and OSINT analysts documented Russian offensive operations throughout the entire ceasefire window, while official channels celebrated the announcement.
